import Mathlib

-- Lemma 1: Every even number greater than 2 can be expressed as a sum of two odd numbers.
theorem even_as_sum_of_odds : ∀ n : ℕ, 2 < n → Even n → ∃ p q : ℕ, Odd p ∧ Odd q ∧ p + q = n := 
begin
  intros n hn he,
  have h1: n > 0, from lt.trans (lt.add_right _ _) hn,
  cases nat.even_odd_of_nat n with hpq hpq,
  { -- Case when n is even
    rcases hpq with ⟨p, q, rfl⟩,
    use p - 1, use q + 1,
    split; try { linarith [h1] },
    split; try { linarith [h1] },
    rw nat.add_sub_cancel_of_le (le_add_one _),
  },
  { -- Case when n is odd
    rcases hpq with ⟨p, q, rfl⟩,
    use p + 1, use q - 1,
    split; try { linarith [h1] },
    split; try { linarith [h1] },
    rw nat.sub_add_cancel_of_le (le_sub_one _),
  }
end

-- Lemma 2: Every odd number greater than 1 is either prime or can be expressed as a product of two smaller natural numbers.
theorem odd_is_prime_or_composite : ∀ n : ℕ, Odd n → 1 < n → (Nat.Prime n ∨ ∃ p q : ℕ, 1 < p ∧ 1 < q ∧ p * q = n) := 
begin
  intros n hn hlt,
  by_cases hp: Nat.Prime n,
  { left, assumption },
  { right,
    have hnp: ¬Nat.Prime n, from mt nat.prime_iff_not_dvd_mul.mp hp,
    rcases nat.exists_factor_pair_of_non_prime n with ⟨p, q, rfl, hp1, hq1, hprod⟩,
    use p, use q,
    split; assumption
  }
end

-- Reduction theorem: Using the above lemmas to approach Goldbach's Conjecture.
theorem reduction : 
  (∀ n : ℕ, 2 < n → Even n → ∃ p q : ℕ, Odd p ∧ Odd q ∧ p + q = n) →
  (∀ n : ℕ, Odd n → 1 < n → (Nat.Prime n ∨ ∃ p q : ℕ, 1 < p ∧ 1 < q ∧ p * q = n)) →
  ∀ n : ℕ, 2 < n → Even n → ∃ p q : ℕ, Nat.Prime p ∧ Nat.Prime q ∧ p + q = n :=
begin
  intros hsum hprime n hn he,
  rcases hsum n hn he with ⟨p, q, hpodd, hqodd, heq⟩,
  cases hprime p (hpodd.elim) (lt_of_lt_of_le (nat.pos_of_odd hpodd) (le_add_one _)) with hp1 hp2,
  { use p, 
    rcases hprime q (hqodd.elim) (lt_of_lt_of_le (nat.pos_of_odd hqodd) (le_add_one _)) with hq1 hq2,
    { use q, split; assumption },
    { rcases hq2 with ⟨r, s, hr1, hs1, heqr⟩,
      have hpr: Nat.Prime r ∨ Nat.Prime s := nat.prime_or_prime_of_mul_eq hpodd.elim hqodd.elim heqr,
      cases hpr with hpr hps,
      { use r, split; assumption },
      { use s, split; assumption } }
  },
  { rcases hp2 with ⟨r, s, hr1, hs1, heqr⟩,
    have hpr: Nat.Prime r ∨ Nat.Prime s := nat.prime_or_prime_of_mul_eq hpodd.elim (nat.even_iff_not_odd.mp (not_not.eta (λ hn, n = p + q ∧ p = r * s ∧ 2 < n ∧ Even n → False))) heqr,
    cases hpr with hpr hps,
    { use r, 
      rcases hprime q (hqodd.elim) (lt_of_lt_of_le (nat.pos_of_odd hqodd) (le_add_one _)) with hq1 hq2,
      { use s, split; assumption },
      { rcases hq2 with ⟨t, u, ht1, hu1, heqt⟩,

        cases hpt with hpt hpu,

import Mathlib

-- Lemma 1: Base Case (n=4)
-- Proves that 4 is the sum of two primes (2 + 2).
theorem lemma_base_case : ∃ p q : ℕ, Nat.Prime p ∧ Nat.Prime q ∧ p + q = 4 := by
  constructor
  . exact 2
  . exact 2
  . exact rfl

-- Lemma 2: Inductive Step (The core assumption)
-- If n-2 is the sum of two primes, then n is also the sum of two primes.
-- This lemma encapsulates the difficulty of the conjecture.
theorem lemma_inductive_step (n : ℕ) (h_n_minus_2 : ∃ p q : ℕ, Nat.Prime p ∧ Nat.Prime q ∧ p + q = n - 2) : ∃ p' q' : ℕ, Nat.Prime p' ∧ Nat.Prime q' ∧ p' + q' = n := by sorry

-- Reduction Theorem
-- Uses the base case (n=4) and the inductive step (n-2 -> n) to prove the target statement.
theorem reduction (H_base : ∃ p q : ℕ, Nat.Prime p ∧ Nat.Prime q ∧ p + q = 4) (H_step : ∀ n : ℕ, (n > 4 → Even n → ∃ p q : ℕ, Nat.Prime p ∧ Nat.Prime q ∧ p + q = n - 2) → ∃ p' q' : ℕ, Nat.Prime p' ∧ Nat.Prime q' ∧ p' + q' = n) :
    ∀ n : ℕ, 2 < n → Even n → ∃ p q : ℕ, Nat.Prime p ∧ Nat.Prime q ∧ p + q = n :=
by
  -- We prove the target statement by induction on k, where n = 2k + 2.
  -- The target is equivalent to proving P(n) for all even n >= 4.
  -- We use the structure of the target statement and induction on n/2.

  let h_target (n : ℕ) (h_n : 2 < n → Even n) : ∃ p q : ℕ, Nat.Prime p ∧ Nat.Prime q ∧ p + q = n :=
    fun n h_n =>
      -- We only care about n such that 2 < n and Even n.
      if 2 < n && Even n then
        if n = 4 then
          -- Base Case: n=4. Use H_base.
          let ⟨p, q, h_p_q⟩ := H_base in
          exact ⟨p, q, h_p_q⟩
        else
          -- Inductive Step: n > 4.
          -- We need to show P(n) assuming P(n-2).
          -- The premise for the inductive step is:
          -- (n > 4 → Even n → P(n-2))
          -- We use the hypothesis H_step, which takes care of this implication.
          let h_n_minus_2_exists := H_step n (by assumption) in
          -- h_n_minus_2_exists is the proof that P(n-2) holds, given the premises.
          -- We use this to conclude P(n).
          let ⟨p', q', h_p'_q'⟩ := h_n_minus_2_exists in
          exact ⟨p', q', h_p'_q'⟩
      else
        -- If the premises (2 < n and Even n) are false, the implication holds vacuously.
        by assumption

  -- The final result is the statement itself, which we have constructed above.
  exact h_target
